    19→namespace Nat

    21→theorem prod_primeFactors_factorization_apply (n : ℕ) {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) {f : ℕ → ℕ → ℕ}
    22→    (hf : ∀ q n, ¬ q ∣ n → f q n = 0) (hf₀ : ∀ q, f q 0 = 0):
    23→    (∏ q ∈ n.primeFactors, q ^ f q n).factorization p = f p n := by
    24→  rw [factorization_prod fun _ _ _ ↦ by simp_all, Finset.sum_congr rfl fun x hx ↦ by
    25→      rw [factorization_pow, (mem_primeFactors.1 hx).1.factorization]]
    26→  by_cases hpn : p ∣ n <;> simp [Finsupp.single_apply, hp, hpn]
    27→  · exact fun _ ↦ by simp_all
    28→  · simp [hf _ _ hpn]
    29→

    17→import Mathlib.Algebra.Order.Ring.Nat
    18→import Mathlib.Algebra.Order.Star.Basic
    19→import Mathlib.Data.Nat.PrimeFin
    20→
    21→namespace Nat
    22→
    23→/--
    24→A natural number $n$ is said to be $k$-full (or $k$-powerful) if for every prime factor $p$ of $n$,
    25→the $k$-th power $p^k$ also divides $n$.
    26→-/
    27→def Full (k : ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop := ∀ p ∈ n.primeFactors, p^k ∣ n
    28→
    29→instance Full.decide : ∀ k n, Decidable (Full k n) := by
    30→  intro k n
    31→  dsimp [Full]
    32→  infer_instance
    33→
    34→/-- Every natural number is $0$-full. -/
    35→theorem Full.zero_left (n : ℕ) : (0 : ℕ).Full n := by
    36→  simp [Nat.Full]
    37→
    38→/-- Every natural number is $1$-full. -/
    39→theorem Full.one_left (n : ℕ) : (1 : ℕ).Full n := by
    40→  aesop (add simp [Nat.Full])
    41→
    42→/-- $0$ is always $k$-full. -/
    43→theorem Full.zero_right (k : ℕ) : k.Full 0 := by
    44→  simp [Nat.Full]
    45→
    46→/-- $1$ is always $k$-full. -/
    47→theorem Full.one_right (k : ℕ) : k.Full 1 := by
    48→  simp [Nat.Full]
    49→
    50→/--
    51→A [Powerful number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Powerful_number) is a natural number $n$ where
    52→for every prime divisor $p$, $p^2$ divides $n$.
    53→Powerful numbers are also known as "squareful", "square-full", or "$2$-full".
    54→-/
    55→abbrev Powerful : ℕ → Prop := (2).Full
    56→
    57→instance Powerful.decide : ∀ n, Decidable (Powerful n) := by
    58→  intro n
    59→  dsimp [Powerful, Full]
    60→  apply Finset.decidableDforallFinset
    61→
    62→theorem full_of_le_full (k : ℕ) (n : ℕ) {m : ℕ} (hk : k ≤ m) (h : m.Full n) : k.Full n :=
    63→  fun p a ↦ pow_dvd_of_le_of_pow_dvd hk (h p a)
    64→
    65→/-- If $n \equiv p \pmod{p ^ (k + 1)}$, for a prime $p$ then $n$ is not $(k + 1)$-full. -/
    66→theorem not_full_of_prime_mod_prime_sq (n : ℕ) (k : ℕ) {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime)
    67→    (h : n % p ^ (k + 1) = p) : ¬ (k + 1).Full n := by
    68→  rw [Full]
    69→  push_neg
    70→  use p
    71→  simp [mem_primeFactors, hp, ne_eq, true_and]
    72→  constructor
    73→  · rw [←Nat.div_add_mod n (p ^ (k + 1)), h]
    74→    have : p ∣ p ^ (k + 1) := by exact Dvd.intro_left (p.pow k) rfl
    75→    simp
    76→    exact ⟨Dvd.dvd.mul_right this (n / p ^ (k + 1)), fun a ↦ Prime.ne_zero hp⟩
    77→  · intro h
    78→    simp_all [Nat.dvd_iff_mod_eq_zero.mp h]
    79→    aesop

    82→open Lean Meta Qq in
    83→/-- Simproc to compute the set `Nat.primeFactors`. -/
    84→dsimproc primeFactorsEq (Nat.primeFactors _) := fun e ↦ do
    85→  unless e.isAppOfArity `Nat.primeFactors 1 do return .continue
    86→  let some n ← fromExpr? e.appArg! | return .continue
    87→  let outAsList : List Q(ℕ) := (unsafe n.primeFactors.val.unquot).map mkNatLit
    88→  let outAsFinset : Q(Finset ℕ) := outAsList.foldl (fun s n ↦ q(insert $n $s)) q({})
    89→  return .done outAsFinset

    18→namespace Nat
    19→
    20→/--
    21→A [perfect power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perfect_power) is a natural number that
    22→  is a product of equal natural factors, or, in other words, an integer that can be expressed
    23→  as a square or a higher integer power of another integer greater than one. More formally,
    24→  $n$ is a perfect power if there exist natural numbers $m > 1$, and $k > 1$ such that
    25→  $m ^ k = n$. In this case, $n$ may be called a perfect $k$th power. If $k = 2$ or
    26→  $k = 3$, then $n$ is called a perfect square or perfect cube, respectively.
    27→-/
    28→def IsPerfectPower (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    29→  ∃ k m : ℕ, 1 < k ∧ 1 < m ∧ k ^ m = n
    30→
    31→theorem isPerfectPower_iff_factorization_gcd (n : ℕ) :
    32→    IsPerfectPower n ↔ n > 1 ∧ n.primeFactors.gcd n.factorization > 1 := by
    33→  constructor
    34→  · -- Forward direction: IsPerfectPower n → n > 1 ∧ gcd > 1
    35→    intro ⟨k, m, hk, hm, hpow⟩
    36→    constructor
    37→    · -- n > 1
    38→      have h : n ≥ 4 := by
    39→        calc n = k ^ m := hpow.symm
    40→          _ ≥ 2 ^ m := Nat.pow_le_pow_left hk m
    41→          _ ≥ 2 ^ 2 := Nat.pow_le_pow_right (by norm_num : 1 ≤ 2) hm
    42→      omega
    43→    · -- gcd > 1
    44→      subst hpow
    45→      have hk_pos : k ≠ 0 := Nat.one_le_iff_ne_zero.mp (le_of_lt hk)
    46→      have hm_pos : m ≠ 0 := Nat.one_le_iff_ne_zero.mp (le_of_lt hm)
    47→      rw [Nat.factorization_pow, primeFactors_pow k hm_pos]
    48→      -- m divides every exponent, so gcd ≥ m > 1
    49→      have h_nonempty := nonempty_primeFactors.mpr hk
    50→      obtain ⟨p, hp⟩ := h_nonempty
    51→      -- Show m divides the gcd
    52→      have h_m_dvd_gcd : m ∣ k.primeFactors.gcd (m • k.factorization) := by
    53→        apply Finset.dvd_gcd
    54→        intro b _
    55→        show m ∣ (m • k.factorization) b
    56→        simp only [Finsupp.coe_smul, Pi.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul]
    57→        exact dvd_mul_right m _
    58→      -- The gcd is positive because k has at least one prime factor with positive exponent
    59→      have hp_in_support : p ∈ k.factorization.support := by
    60→        rw [support_factorization]; exact hp
    61→      have hp_exp_pos : 0 < k.factorization p := Finsupp.mem_support_iff.mp hp_in_support |> Nat.pos_of_ne_zero
    62→      have h_gcd_pos : 0 < k.primeFactors.gcd (m • k.factorization) := by
    63→        have h1 := Finset.gcd_dvd (f := (m • k.factorization)) hp
    64→        have h_eq : (m • k.factorization) p = m * k.factorization p := by
    65→          simp only [Finsupp.coe_smul, Pi.smul_apply, smul_eq_mul]
    66→        rw [h_eq] at h1
    67→        have h_val_pos : 0 < m * k.factorization p := Nat.mul_pos (Nat.pos_of_ne_zero hm_pos) hp_exp_pos
    68→        exact Nat.pos_of_dvd_of_pos h1 h_val_pos
    69→      exact Nat.lt_of_lt_of_le hm (Nat.le_of_dvd h_gcd_pos h_m_dvd_gcd)
    70→  · -- Backward direction: n > 1 ∧ gcd > 1 → IsPerfectPower n
    71→    intro ⟨hn, hgcd⟩
    72→    set g := n.primeFactors.gcd n.factorization with hg_def
    73→    -- Define k as the product of p^(n.factorization p / g) for all primes p | n
    74→    let f : ℕ → ℕ := fun q => q ^ (n.factorization q / g)
    75→    set k := n.primeFactors.prod f with hk_def
    76→    use k, g
    77→    refine ⟨?_, hgcd, ?_⟩
    78→    · -- k > 1
    79→      have h_nonempty := nonempty_primeFactors.mpr hn
    80→      obtain ⟨p, hp⟩ := h_nonempty
    81→      have hp_prime := prime_of_mem_primeFactors hp
    82→      -- p ∈ n.primeFactors means n.factorization p > 0
    83→      have hp_in_support : p ∈ n.factorization.support := by
    84→        rw [support_factorization]; exact hp
    85→      have hp_pos : 0 < n.factorization p := Finsupp.mem_support_iff.mp hp_in_support |> Nat.pos_of_ne_zero
    86→      have h_div : g ∣ n.factorization p := Finset.gcd_dvd hp
    87→      have hg_pos : 0 < g := Nat.lt_trans Nat.zero_lt_one hgcd
    88→      have h_quot_pos : 0 < n.factorization p / g :=
    89→        Nat.div_pos (Nat.le_of_dvd hp_pos h_div) hg_pos
    90→      have h_ge_p : k ≥ f p := by
    91→        rw [hk_def]
    92→        exact Finset.single_le_prod' (f := f)
    93→          (fun q hq => Nat.one_le_pow _ _ (Prime.one_le (prime_of_mem_primeFactors hq))) hp
    94→      have h_ge_2 : f p ≥ 2 := by
    95→        show p ^ (n.factorization p / g) ≥ 2
    96→        calc p ^ (n.factorization p / g) ≥ p ^ 1 := Nat.pow_le_pow_right (Prime.one_le hp_prime) h_quot_pos
    97→          _ = p := pow_one p
    98→          _ ≥ 2 := Prime.two_le hp_prime
    99→      omega
   100→    · -- k ^ g = n
   101→      have h_eq : k ^ g = n.primeFactors.prod (fun q => q ^ (n.factorization q / g * g)) := by
   102→        rw [hk_def, ← Finset.prod_pow]
   103→        congr 1
   104→        ext q
   105→        show (f q) ^ g = q ^ (n.factorization q / g * g)
   106→        simp only [f, ← pow_mul]
   107→      rw [h_eq]
   108→      have hn_ne_zero : n ≠ 0 := Nat.ne_of_gt (Nat.lt_of_succ_lt hn)
   109→      conv_rhs => rw [← Nat.factorization_prod_pow_eq_self hn_ne_zero]
   110→      congr 1
   111→      ext p
   112→      by_cases hp : p ∈ n.primeFactors
   113→      · have h_div : g ∣ n.factorization p := Finset.gcd_dvd hp
   114→        rw [Nat.div_mul_cancel h_div]
   115→      · -- p ∉ n.primeFactors means n.factorization p = 0
   116→        have hp_not_in_support : p ∉ n.factorization.support := by
   117→          rw [support_factorization]; exact hp
   118→        have hp_zero : n.factorization p = 0 := Finsupp.notMem_support_iff.mp hp_not_in_support
   119→        simp only [hp_zero, Nat.zero_div, zero_mul, pow_zero]
   120→
   121→instance IsPerfectPower.decide : ∀ n, Decidable (IsPerfectPower n) := fun n =>
   122→  decidable_of_iff (n > 1 ∧ n.primeFactors.gcd n.factorization > 1)
   123→    (isPerfectPower_iff_factorization_gcd n).symm
   124→
   125→example : IsPerfectPower 4 := by native_decide
   126→example : IsPerfectPower 27 := by native_decide
   127→example : ¬IsPerfectPower 0 := by native_decide
   128→example : ¬IsPerfectPower 1 := by native_decide
   129→example : ¬IsPerfectPower 2 := by native_decide
   130→
   131→end Nat
   132→

    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Squarefree
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Primitive elements of a set with respect to divisibility
    21→
    22→An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n` is in `S`.
    23→
    24→This concept appears naturally when studying sequences closed under "divisor inheritance" -
    25→if every member `n` of a sequence has the property that `m * n` is also in the sequence
    26→for coprime `m`, then the primitive elements generate the entire sequence.
    27→
    28→## Main definitions
    29→
    30→* `Set.IsPrimitive`: A natural number `n` is primitive in `S` if `n ∈ S` and
    31→  `n.properDivisors` is disjoint from `S`.
    32→* `Set.primitives`: The set of primitive elements of `S`.
    33→
    34→## Main results
    35→
    36→* `Set.IsPrimitive.mem`: A primitive element is in the set.
    37→* `Set.IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor`: Proper divisors of a primitive element are not
    38→  in the set.
    39→* `Set.one_isPrimitive_iff`: `1` is primitive in `S` iff `1 ∈ S`.
    40→* `Set.prime_isPrimitive_iff`: A prime `p` is primitive in `S` iff `p ∈ S` and `1 ∉ S`.
    41→-/
    42→
    43→namespace Set
    44→
    45→/-- An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n`
    46→is in `S`. -/
    47→def IsPrimitive (S : Set ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    48→  n ∈ S ∧ Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ℕ) S
    49→
    50→/-- The set of primitive elements of `S`. -/
    51→def primitives (S : Set ℕ) : Set ℕ :=
    52→  {n | S.IsPrimitive n}
    53→
    54→theorem IsPrimitive.mem {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) : n ∈ S := h.1
    55→
    56→theorem IsPrimitive.disjoint_properDivisors {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) :
    57→    Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ℕ) S := h.2
    58→
    59→theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor {S : Set ℕ} {n d : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    60→    (hd : d ∈ n.properDivisors) : d ∉ S :=
    61→  Set.disjoint_left.mp h.2 hd
    62→
    63→theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_dvd_of_lt {S : Set ℕ} {n d : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    64→    (hdvd : d ∣ n) (hlt : d < n) : d ∉ S :=
    65→  h.not_mem_of_properDivisor (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr ⟨hdvd, hlt⟩)
    66→
    67→theorem isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} :
    68→    S.IsPrimitive n ↔ n ∈ S ∧ ∀ d ∈ n.properDivisors, d ∉ S := by
    69→  simp only [IsPrimitive, Set.disjoint_left, Finset.mem_coe]
    70→
    71→theorem isPrimitive_iff' {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (_hn : 0 < n) :
    72→    S.IsPrimitive n ↔ n ∈ S ∧ ∀ d, d ∣ n → d < n → d ∉ S := by
    73→  rw [isPrimitive_iff]
    74→  constructor <;> intro ⟨hmem, hdiv⟩
    75→  · exact ⟨hmem, fun d hd hlt => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr ⟨hd, hlt⟩)⟩
    76→  · exact ⟨hmem, fun d hd => hdiv d (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).1 (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mp hd).2⟩
    77→
    78→theorem mem_primitives_iff {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} : n ∈ S.primitives ↔ S.IsPrimitive n := Iff.rfl
    79→
    80→@[simp]
    81→theorem primitives_subset {S : Set ℕ} : S.primitives ⊆ S := fun _ h => h.mem
    82→
    83→theorem one_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ℕ} : S.IsPrimitive 1 ↔ 1 ∈ S := by
    84→  simp [isPrimitive_iff, Nat.properDivisors_one]
    85→
    86→theorem prime_isPrimitive_iff {S : Set ℕ} {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) :
    87→    S.IsPrimitive p ↔ p ∈ S ∧ 1 ∉ S := by
    88→  simp [isPrimitive_iff, hp.properDivisors]
    89→
    90→end Set
    91→

    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Squarefree
    18→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Data.Nat.Factorization.Basic
    19→
    20→namespace Nat
    21→
    22→/-- `n.squarefreePart` is the unique `a₀ : ℕ` such that `a₀` is squarefree and `n = a₀ * b ^ 2`,
    23→for some `b : ℕ`. At `0` this property is not well defined because `b` must be `0` and `a₀` can be
    24→any squarefree number; we give the junk value `1` at `n = 0` following the convention that the
    25→squarefree part of any square is `1`. -/
    26→def squarefreePart (n : ℕ) : ℕ := n.factorization.prod fun (p e : ℕ) ↦ p ^ (e % 2)
    27→
    28→example : squarefreePart 2 = 2 := by
    29→  decide +native
    30→
    31→example : squarefreePart 5 = 5 := by
    32→  decide +native
    33→
    34→example : squarefreePart 4 = 1 := by
    35→  decide +native
    36→
    37→example : squarefreePart 8 = 2 := by
    38→  decide +native
    39→
    40→example : squarefreePart 16 = 1 := by
    41→  decide +native
    42→
    43→example : squarefreePart 24 = 6 := by
    44→  decide +native
    45→
    46→theorem squarefreePart_ne_zero (n : ℕ) : n.squarefreePart ≠ 0 := by
    47→  simp [squarefreePart]
    48→
    49→theorem squarefreePart_zero : squarefreePart 0 = 1 := by
    50→  simp [squarefreePart]
    51→
    52→/-- If `n` is squarefree, then its squarefree part is itself. -/
    53→theorem squarefreePart_of_squarefree {n : ℕ} (hn : Squarefree n) :
    54→    squarefreePart n = n := by
    55→  nth_rw 2 [← n.factorization_prod_pow_eq_self fun _ ↦ by simp_all]
    56→  simp only [squarefreePart, Finsupp.prod, support_factorization]
    57→  exact Finset.prod_congr rfl fun p hp ↦ by
    58→    rw [factorization_eq_one_of_squarefree hn (mem_primeFactors.1 hp).1 (mem_primeFactors.1 hp).2.1]
    59→
    60→/-- The squarefree part of any square is `1`. -/
    61→theorem squarefreePart_of_isSquare {n : ℕ} (hn : IsSquare n) :
    62→    squarefreePart n = 1 := by
    63→  rcases eq_or_ne n 0 with (rfl | h₀); exact squarefreePart_zero
    64→  obtain ⟨r, rfl⟩ := hn
    65→  rw [ne_eq, mul_eq_zero, or_self] at h₀
    66→  rw [squarefreePart, Finsupp.prod, support_factorization,
    67→    Finset.prod_congr rfl fun p hp ↦ by rw [r.factorization_mul h₀ h₀]]
    68→  simp [← two_mul]
    69→
    70→/-- If $n = \prod_p p^{e_p}$ is the prime factorization of $n$, then $\prod_p p^{e_p \pmod{2}}$
    71→is the prime factorization of the squarefree part of $n$. -/
    72→theorem squarefreePart_factorization (n : ℕ) {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) :
    73→    n.squarefreePart.factorization p = n.factorization p % 2 := by
    74→  rw [squarefreePart, prod_factorization_eq_prod_primeFactors,
    75→    n.prod_primeFactors_factorization_apply hp (f := fun p n ↦ n.factorization p % 2)
    76→      (fun _ _ h ↦ by simp [factorization_eq_zero_of_not_dvd h]) (by simp)]
    77→
    78→theorem Prime.squarefree {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) : Squarefree p := Irreducible.squarefree hp
    79→
    80→theorem squarefree_squarefreePart (n : ℕ) : Squarefree n.squarefreePart := by
    81→  refine Nat.squarefree_iff_factorization_le_one n.squarefreePart_ne_zero |>.2 fun p ↦ ?_
    82→  by_cases hp : p.Prime
    83→  · linarith [n.squarefreePart_factorization hp, Nat.mod_lt (n.factorization p) two_pos]
    84→  · linarith [factorization_eq_zero_of_not_prime n.squarefreePart hp]
    85→
    86→theorem squarefreePart_dvd (n : ℕ) : squarefreePart n ∣ n := by
    87→  rcases eq_or_ne n 0 with (rfl | h₀); simp
    88→  exact Nat.factorization_prime_le_iff_dvd n.squarefreePart_ne_zero h₀ |>.1 fun p hp ↦
    89→    squarefreePart_factorization _ hp ▸ Nat.mod_le _ _
    90→
    91→/-- The square part is the value of `b ^ 2` in the squarefree decomposition of `n = a₀ * b ^ 2`. -/
    92→def squarePart (n : ℕ) : ℕ := n / n.squarefreePart
    93→
    94→theorem squarePart_zero : squarePart 0 = 0 := by simp [squarePart]
    95→
    96→/-- The squarefree decomposition of a natural number. -/
    97→theorem squarefreePart_mul_squarePart (n : ℕ) : n.squarefreePart * n.squarePart = n :=
    98→  Nat.mul_div_eq_iff_dvd.2 n.squarefreePart_dvd
    99→
   100→theorem squarefree_infinite : Set.Infinite { n : ℕ | Squarefree n } :=
   101→  Set.Infinite.mono (fun _ hp ↦ hp.squarefree) Nat.infinite_setOf_prime
   102→
   103→end Nat
   104→

    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Data.Nat.PrimeFin
    18→import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Lattice
    19→
    20→namespace Nat
    21→
    22→/-- The greatest prime divisor of a natural number `n > 1`.
    23→
    24→Takes the junk value `0` for `n = 0` and `n = 1`.
    25→
    26→TODO Consider redefining this using the computable implementation:
    27→`n.primeFactorsList.getLastI`. -/
    28→noncomputable def maxPrimeFac (n : ℕ) : ℕ := sSup {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n}
    29→
    30→@[simp]
    31→lemma maxPrimeFac_zero :
    32→    maxPrimeFac 0 = 0 := by
    33→  simpa [maxPrimeFac] using Set.Infinite.Nat.sSup_eq_zero infinite_setOf_prime
    34→
    35→@[simp]
    36→lemma maxPrimeFac_one :
    37→    maxPrimeFac 1 = 0 := by
    38→  suffices {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p = 1} = ∅ by simp [maxPrimeFac, this]
    39→  aesop
    40→
    41→lemma prime_maxPrimeFac_of_one_lt (n : ℕ) (h : 1 < n) :
    42→    Prime (maxPrimeFac n) := by
    43→  set s := {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n} with hs
    44→  have hs₀ : s.Nonempty := by
    45→    simp only [Set.Nonempty, Set.mem_setOf_eq, ← ne_one_iff_exists_prime_dvd, hs]
    46→    omega
    47→  have hs₁ : BddAbove s := by
    48→    use n
    49→    simp only [hs, mem_upperBounds, Set.mem_setOf_eq, and_imp]
    50→    exact fun p _ hp ↦ Nat.le_of_dvd (zero_lt_of_lt h) hp
    51→  exact (Nat.sSup_mem hs₀ hs₁).1
    52→
    53→lemma maxPrimeFac_eq_of_dvd_of_le
    54→    (n p : ℕ) (hn : 0 < n) (hp : p.Prime) (h_dvd : p ∣ n) (h_le : maxPrimeFac n ≤ p) :
    55→    maxPrimeFac n = p := by
    56→  refine le_antisymm h_le ?_
    57→  replace hn : 1 < n := Nat.lt_of_lt_of_le hp.one_lt (Nat.le_of_dvd hn h_dvd)
    58→  set s := {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n} with hs
    59→  have hs₁ : BddAbove s := by
    60→    use n
    61→    simp only [hs, mem_upperBounds, Set.mem_setOf_eq, and_imp]
    62→    exact fun p _ hp ↦ Nat.le_of_dvd (zero_lt_of_lt hn) hp
    63→  exact ConditionallyCompleteLattice.le_csSup s p hs₁ ⟨hp, h_dvd⟩
    64→
    65→@[simp]
    66→lemma one_lt_maxPrimeFac_iff (n : ℕ) :
    67→    1 < maxPrimeFac n ↔ 1 < n := by
    68→  rcases lt_trichotomy n 1 with hn | rfl | hn
    69→  · simp only [lt_one_iff] at hn
    70→    simp [hn]
    71→  · simp
    72→  · simpa [hn] using (prime_maxPrimeFac_of_one_lt n hn).one_lt
    73→
    74→end Nat
    75→

    16→import Mathlib.Algebra.Group.Pointwise.Set.Basic
    17→import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.Submodule.Lattice
    18→import Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Defs
    19→
    20→open Pointwise
    21→
    22→/--
    23→A covering system of a semiring `R` is a finite set of cosets of non-zero ideals whose union
    24→covers the whole ring. In the case where `R = ℤ`, this corresponds to a choice of finitely many
    25→congruence relations such that every integer satisfies at least one of the relations.
    26→-/
    27→structure CoveringSystem (R : Type*) [CommSemiring R] where
    28→  ι : Type
    29→  [fintypeIndex : Fintype ι]
    30→  residue : ι → R
    31→  moduli : ι → Ideal R
    32→  unionCovers : ⋃ i, ({residue i} : Set R) + (moduli i : Set R) = @Set.univ R
    33→  non_trivial : ∀ i, moduli i ≠ ⊥
    34→
    35→/--
    36→We say a covering system is strict if all the congruence relations that define it are take modulo
    37→a different ideal (or number).
    38→
    39→Note: this corresponds to the notion of a covering system that Erdos was using in
    40→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number theory. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    41→-/
    42→structure StrictCoveringSystem (R : Type*) [CommSemiring R] extends CoveringSystem R where
    43→  injective_moduli : moduli.Injective
    44→
    45→namespace CoveringSystem
    46→
    47→variable {R : Type*} [CommSemiring R]
    48→
    49→def coset (c : CoveringSystem R) (i : c.ι) : Set R :=
    50→  {c.residue i} + c.moduli i
    51→
    52→@[simp]
    53→theorem iUnion_cosets (c : CoveringSystem R) : ⋃ i, c.coset i = Set.univ :=
    54→  c.unionCovers
    55→
    56→end CoveringSystem
    57→

    24→*References:*
    25→ - [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beurling_zeta_function)
    26→ - [Beurling Zeta Functions, Generalised Primes, and Fractal Membranes](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0410270)
    27→-/
    28→
    29→open Filter
    30→
    31→/-- A sequence of real numbers `1 < a 0 < a 1 < ...` is called a set of Beurling prime numbers if
    32→it tends to infinity. -/
    33→noncomputable def IsBeurlingPrimes (a : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    34→  1 < a 0 ∧ StrictMono a ∧ Tendsto a atTop atTop
    35→
    36→/-- The set of Beurling integers are numbers of the form `∏ i, (a i) ^ (k i)`, where `k` has
    37→finite support. -/
    38→def BeurlingInteger (a : ℕ → ℝ) : Set ℝ :=
    39→  letI g : (ℕ →₀ ℕ) → ℝ := fun k => k.prod (fun x y => (a x) ^ y)
    40→  .range g
    41→
    42→/-- Every element of the sequence `a` is a Beurling integer. -/
    43→lemma generator_mem_beurling (a : ℕ → ℝ) (i : ℕ) : a i ∈ BeurlingInteger a :=
    44→  ⟨Finsupp.single i 1, by aesop⟩
    45→
    46→/-- The set of Beurling integers is closed under multiplication. -/
    47→lemma mul_mem_beurling {a : ℕ → ℝ} {x y : ℝ} (hx : x ∈ BeurlingInteger a) (hy : y ∈ BeurlingInteger a) :
    48→    x * y ∈ BeurlingInteger a := by
    49→  obtain ⟨k, rfl⟩ := hx
    50→  obtain ⟨l, rfl⟩ := hy
    51→  exact ⟨k + l, by simp [Finsupp.prod_add_index', pow_add]⟩
    52→
    53→/-- The set of Beurling integers is closed under taking powers. -/
    54→lemma pow_mem_beurling {a : ℕ → ℝ} {x : ℝ} (k : ℕ) (hx : x ∈ BeurlingInteger a) :
    55→    x ^ k ∈ BeurlingInteger a := by
    56→  induction k with
    57→  | zero => exact ⟨0, by norm_num⟩
    58→  | succ k ih => simpa [pow_succ'] using mul_mem_beurling hx ih
    59→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 915
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/915](https://www.erdosproblems.com/915)
    23→
    24→Let $G$ be a graph with $1 + n(m-1)$ vertices and $1 + n\binom{m}{2}$ edges.
    25→Must $G$ contain two vertices which are connected by $m$ disjoint paths?
    26→
    27→A conjecture of Bollobás and Erdős [BoEr62]. It is unclear whether "disjoint"
    28→means edge-disjoint or (internally) vertex-disjoint. The construction of $n$
    29→copies of $K_m$ sharing a single vertex is valid for either interpretation.
    30→
    31→The vertex-disjoint version was disproved by Leonard [Le73] for $m = 5$ and by
    32→Mader [Ma73] for all $m \geq 6$. The edge-disjoint version was confirmed (and
    33→strengthened) by Mader [Ma73].
    34→
    35→[BoEr62] Bollobás, B. and Erdős, P., 1962.
    36→
    37→[Le73] Leonard, 1973.
    38→
    39→[Ma73] Mader, W., 1973.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open SimpleGraph Finset
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos915
    45→
    46→/-- Two vertices $u$, $v$ in a simple graph $G$ are connected by $m$ internally
    47→vertex-disjoint paths if there exist $m$ paths from $u$ to $v$ such that
    48→no internal vertex of one path appears as an internal vertex of another. -/
    49→def HasInternallyDisjointPaths {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V)
    50→    (u v : V) (m : ℕ) : Prop :=
    51→  ∃ (paths : Fin m → G.Walk u v),
    52→    (∀ i, (paths i).IsPath) ∧
    53→    (∀ i j, i ≠ j → ∀ w : V, w ≠ u → w ≠ v →
    54→      w ∈ (paths i).support → w ∉ (paths j).support)
    55→
    56→/--
    57→Erdős Problem 915 (Bollobás–Erdős conjecture [BoEr62]):
    58→
    59→Let $G$ be a graph with $1 + n(m-1)$ vertices and at least $1 + n\binom{m}{2}$ edges.
    60→Must $G$ contain two vertices connected by $m$ internally vertex-disjoint paths?
    61→
    62→This was disproved by Leonard [Le73] for $m = 5$ and by Mader [Ma73] for all
    63→$m \geq 6$. The edge-disjoint analogue was confirmed by Mader.
    64→-/
    65→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    66→theorem erdos_915 : answer(False) ↔
    67→    ∀ (n m : ℕ), m ≥ 2 →
    68→    ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin (1 + n * (m - 1)))) [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    69→    G.edgeFinset.card ≥ 1 + n * m.choose 2 →
    70→    ∃ u v : Fin (1 + n * (m - 1)), u ≠ v ∧
    71→      HasInternallyDisjointPaths G u v m := by
    72→  sorry
    73→
    74→end Erdos915
    75→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2025 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→

     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.

     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Acyclic
    18→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Bipartite
    19→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Matching
    20→import Mathlib.Data.Real.Archimedean
    21→import Mathlib.Analysis.InnerProductSpace.PiL2
    22→
    23→namespace SimpleGraph
    24→
    25→open Classical Finset List
    26→
    27→variable {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
    28→
    29→/-- `Ls G` is the maximum number of leaves over all spanning trees of `G`.
    30→It is defined to be `0` when `G` is not connected. -/
    31→noncomputable def Ls (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    32→  letI spanningTrees := { T : Subgraph G | T.IsSpanning ∧ IsTree T.coe }
    33→  letI leaves (T : Subgraph G) := T.verts.toFinset.filter (fun v => T.degree v = 1)
    34→  letI num_leaves (T : Subgraph G) := (leaves T).card
    35→  sSup (Set.image (fun T => (num_leaves T : ℝ)) spanningTrees)
    36→
    37→/-- `n G` is the number of vertices of `G` as a real number. -/
    38→noncomputable def n (_ : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := Fintype.card α
    39→
    40→/-- `m G` is the size of a maximum matching of `G`. -/
    41→noncomputable def m (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    42→  let matchings := { M : Subgraph G | M.IsMatching }
    43→  sSup (Set.image (fun M => (M.edgeSet.toFinset.card : ℝ)) matchings)
    44→
    45→/-- The maximum cardinality among all independent sets `s`
    46→    that maximize the quantity `|s| - |N(s)|`, where `N(s)`
    47→    is the neighborhood of the set `s`. -/
    48→noncomputable def aprime (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    49→  letI indep_sets : Finset (Finset α) := univ.powerset.filter (fun s => G.IsIndepSet (s : Set α))
    50→  letI diff (s : Finset α) : ℤ := (s.card : ℤ) - (⋃ v ∈ (s : Set α), G.neighborSet v).toFinset.card
    51→  letI max_diff := (indep_sets.image diff).max
    52→  letI critical_sets := indep_sets.filter (fun s ↦ diff s = max_diff.getD 0)
    53→  letI max_card := (critical_sets.image Finset.card).max
    54→  (max_card.getD 0 : ℝ)
    55→
    56→/-- `largestInducedForestSize G` is the size of a largest induced forest of `G`. -/
    57→noncomputable def largestInducedForestSize (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    58→  sSup { n | ∃ s : Finset α, (G.induce s).IsAcyclic ∧ s.card = n }
    59→
    60→/-- The degree sequence of a graph, sorted in nondecreasing order. -/
    61→noncomputable def degreeSequence (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : List ℕ :=
    62→  (Finset.univ.val.map fun v : α => G.degree v).sort (· ≤ ·)
    63→
    64→/--
    65→The maximum number of occurrences of any term of the degree sequence of `G`.
    66→-/
    67→noncomputable def degreeSequenceMultiplicity (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
    68→  letI degs := degreeSequence G
    69→  (List.max? (degs.map (fun d => degs.count d))).getD 0
    70→
    71→/-- `largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize G` is the size of a largest induced
    72→bipartite subgraph of `G`. -/
    73→noncomputable def largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    74→  sSup { n | ∃ s : Finset α, (G.induce s).IsBipartite ∧ s.card = n }
    75→
    76→/-- `b G` is the number of vertices of a largest induced bipartite subgraph of `G`.
    77→Returned as a real number. -/
    78→noncomputable def b (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    79→  (largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize G : ℝ)
    80→
    81→/-- Independence number of the neighbourhood of `v`. -/
    82→noncomputable def indepNeighborsCard (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℕ :=
    83→  (G.induce (G.neighborSet v)).indepNum
    84→
    85→/-- The same quantity as a real number. -/
    86→noncomputable def indepNeighbors (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℝ :=
    87→  (indepNeighborsCard G v : ℝ)
    88→
    89→/-- Average of `indepNeighbors` over all vertices. -/
    90→noncomputable def averageIndepNeighbors (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    91→  (∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, indepNeighbors G v) / (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
    92→
    93→/-- A unit distance graph in ℝ²:
    94→A graph where the vertices V are a collection of points in ℝ² and there is
    95→an edge between two points if and only if the distance between them is 1. -/
    96→def UnitDistancePlaneGraph (V : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : SimpleGraph V where
    97→  Adj x y := Dist.dist x y = 1
    98→  symm x y := by simp [PseudoMetricSpace.dist_comm]
    99→
   100→/--
   101→Two walks are internally disjoint if they share no vertices other than their endpoints.
   102→-/
   103→def InternallyDisjoint {V : Type*} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v x y : V}
   104→    (p : G.Walk u v) (q : G.Walk x y) : Prop :=
   105→  Disjoint p.support.tail.dropLast q.support.tail.dropLast
   106→
   107→/--
   108→We say a graph is infinitely connected if any two vertices are connected by infinitely many
   109→pairwise disjoint paths.
   110→-/
   111→def InfinitelyConnected {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
   112→  Pairwise fun u v ↦ ∃ P : Set (G.Walk u v),
   113→    P.Infinite ∧ (∀ p ∈ P, p.IsPath) ∧ P.Pairwise InternallyDisjoint
   114→
   115→/-- Infinite graphs: definitions for max degree and clique number so that the maximum
   116→degree (resp. clique number) of a graph with unbounded degree (resp. clique size) is
   117→`∞` rather than 0.
   118→-/
   119→noncomputable
   120→def edegree {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (v : V) : ℕ∞ := (G.neighborSet v).encard
   121→
   122→noncomputable
   123→def emaxDegree {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ∞ := ⨆ v, G.edegree v
   124→
   125→noncomputable
   126→def ecliqueNum {V : Type} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ∞ := ⨆ (s : Finset V) (_ : G.IsClique s), #s
   127→
   128→end SimpleGraph
   129→

    22→
    23→noncomputable def Matrix.IsHermitian.maxEigenvalue {𝕜 : Type*} [Field 𝕜] [RCLike 𝕜]
    24→    {n : Type*} [Fintype n] [DecidableEq n] {A : Matrix n n 𝕜} (hA : A.IsHermitian) : ℝ :=
    25→  iSup hA.eigenvalues
    26→
    27→namespace SimpleGraph
    28→
    29→open Classical
    30→
    31→variable {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
    32→
    33→/-- Abbreviation for the average independence number of the neighborhoods.
    34→-/
    35→noncomputable abbrev l (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := averageIndepNeighbors G
    36→
    37→/-- The same quantity under a different name, used in some conjectures.
    38→-/
    39→noncomputable abbrev l_avg (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := averageIndepNeighbors G
    40→
    41→/-- Independent domination number of `G`. -/
    42→noncomputable def gi (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ := G.indepDominationNumber
    43→
    44→/-- `temp_v G v = deg(v)/(n(G) - deg(v))`. -/
    45→noncomputable def temp_v (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (v : α) : ℝ :=
    46→  let n := Fintype.card α
    47→  let deg := G.degree v
    48→  if n = deg then 0 else (deg : ℝ) / ((n : ℝ) - (deg : ℝ))
    49→
    50→/-- Maximum of `temp_v` over all vertices. -/
    51→noncomputable def MaxTemp (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] [Fintype α] [Nonempty α] : ℝ :=
    52→  let temps := Finset.univ.image (temp_v G)
    53→  temps.max' (Finset.image_nonempty.mpr Finset.univ_nonempty)
    54→
    55→/-- Cardinality of the union of the neighbourhoods of the ends of the non-edge `e`. -/
    56→def non_edge_neighborhood_card (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (e : Sym2 α) : ℕ :=
    57→  Sym2.lift ⟨fun u v => (G.neighborFinset u ∪ G.neighborFinset v).card,
    58→    fun u v => by simp [Finset.union_comm]⟩ e
    59→
    60→/-- Minimum size of the neighbourhood of a non-edge of `G`. -/
    61→noncomputable def NG (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    62→  let non_edges := (compl G).edgeFinset
    63→  if h : non_edges.Nonempty then
    64→    let neighbor_sizes := non_edges.image (non_edge_neighborhood_card G)
    65→    (neighbor_sizes.min' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _))
    66→  else
    67→    (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
    68→
    69→/-- Size of a largest induced forest of `G` expressed as a real number. -/
    70→noncomputable def S (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    71→  let card := Fintype.card α
    72→  if card < 2 then 0 else
    73→    let degrees := Multiset.ofList (List.map (fun v => G.degree v) Finset.univ.toList)
    74→    let sorted_degrees := degrees.sort (· ≤ ·)
    75→    ↑((sorted_degrees[card - 2]?).getD 0)
    76→
    77→/-- Local eccentricity of a vertex. -/
    78→noncomputable def local_eccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ENat :=
    79→  sSup (Set.range (G.edist v))
    80→
    81→/-- The largest integer less than or equal to `x`. -/
    82→noncomputable def FLOOR (x : ℝ) : ℝ := ⌊x⌋
    83→
    84→/-- Eccentricity of a vertex. -/
    85→noncomputable def eccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℕ∞ :=
    86→  sSup (Set.range (G.edist v))
    87→
    88→/-- The minimum eccentricity among all vertices. -/
    89→noncomputable def minEccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ∞ :=
    90→  sInf (Set.range (eccentricity G))
    91→
    92→/-- The set of vertices of minimum eccentricity. -/
    93→noncomputable def graphCenter (G : SimpleGraph α) : Set α :=
    94→  {v : α | eccentricity G v = minEccentricity G}
    95→
    96→/-- The maximum eccentricity among all vertices. -/
    97→noncomputable def maxEccentricity (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ∞ :=
    98→  sSup (Set.range (eccentricity G))
    99→
   100→/-- The set of vertices of maximum eccentricity. -/
   101→noncomputable def maxEccentricityVertices (G : SimpleGraph α) : Set α :=
   102→  {v : α | eccentricity G v = maxEccentricity G}
   103→
   104→/-- Distance from a vertex to a finite set. -/
   105→noncomputable def distToSet (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) (S : Set α) : ℕ :=
   106→  if h : S.toFinset.Nonempty then
   107→    (S.toFinset.image (fun s => G.dist v s)).min' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _)
   108→  else 0
   109→
   110→/-- Average distance of `G`. -/
   111→noncomputable def averageDistance (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
   112→  if Fintype.card α > 1 then
   113→    (∑ u ∈ Finset.univ, ∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, (G.dist u v : ℝ)) /
   114→      ((Fintype.card α : ℝ) * ((Fintype.card α : ℝ) - 1))
   115→  else
   116→    0
   117→
   118→/-- The floor of the average distance of `G`. -/
   119→noncomputable def path (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   120→  if G.Connected then
   121→    Nat.floor (averageDistance G)
   122→  else
   123→    0
   124→
   125→/-- Auxiliary quantity `ecc` used in conjecture 34. -/
   126→noncomputable def ecc (G : SimpleGraph α) (S : Set α) : ℕ :=
   127→  let s_comp := Finset.univ.filter (fun v => v ∉ S)
   128→  if h : s_comp.Nonempty then
   129→    (s_comp.image (fun v => distToSet G v S)).max' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _)
   130→  else 0
   131→
   132→/-- Average distance from all vertices to a given set. -/
   133→noncomputable def distavg (G : SimpleGraph α) (S : Set α) : ℝ :=
   134→  if Fintype.card α > 0 then
   135→    (∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, (distToSet G v S : ℝ)) / (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
   136→  else
   137→    0
   138→
   139→/-- A family of paths covering all vertices without overlaps. -/
   140→def IsPathCover (G : SimpleGraph α) (P : Finset (Finset α)) : Prop :=
   141→  (∀ s1 ∈ P, ∀ s2 ∈ P, s1 ≠ s2 → Disjoint s1 s2) ∧
   142→  (Finset.univ ⊆ P.biUnion id) ∧
   143→  (∀ s ∈ P, ∃ (u v : α) (p : G.Walk u v), p.IsPath ∧ s = p.support.toFinset)
   144→
   145→/-- Minimum size of a path cover of `G`. -/
   146→noncomputable def pathCoverNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   147→  sInf { k | ∃ P : Finset (Finset α), P.card = k ∧ IsPathCover G P }
   148→
   149→/-- The same quantity as a real number. -/
   150→noncomputable def p (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := (pathCoverNumber G : ℝ)
   151→
   152→/-- The Wiener index of `G`, which is the sum of distances between all
   153→pairs of vertices. -/
   154→noncomputable def wienerIndex (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
   155→  ∑ uv : Sym2 α, uv.lift ⟨fun u v ↦ G.dist u v, by simp [dist_comm]⟩
   156→
   157→/-- Auxiliary function for Szeged index: counts vertices closer to u than v. -/
   158→noncomputable def szeged_aux (G : SimpleGraph α) (u v : α) : ℕ :=
   159→  (Finset.univ.filter (fun w => G.edist w u < G.edist w v)).card
   160→
   161→/-- The Szeged index of `G`.
   162→
   163→This is define as the sum `∑_{uv ∈ E(G)} n_u(u,v) * n_v(u,v)` where
   164→`n_u(uv)` is the number of vertices closer to `u` than `v`.
   165→-/
   166→noncomputable def szegedIndex (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   167→  ∑ e ∈ G.edgeFinset,
   168→    e.lift ⟨fun u v => szeged_aux G u v * szeged_aux G v u, by simp [mul_comm]⟩
   169→
   170→/-- The average degree of `G`. -/
   171→noncomputable def averageDegree (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℚ  :=
   172→  (∑ v, (G.degree v : ℚ)) / (Fintype.card α : ℚ)
   173→
   174→/-- The multiset of degrees of a graph. -/
   175→def degreeMultiset (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : Multiset ℕ :=
   176→  Finset.univ.val.map fun v => G.degree v
   177→
   178→/-- The annihilation number of a graph. This is the largest number of degrees that can be added
   179→together without going over the total number of edges of that graph. -/
   180→def annihilationNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   181→  -- Calculate the limit: The number of edges (Sum of degrees / 2)
   182→  letI limit := G.edgeFinset.card
   183→
   184→  -- The set of all multisets of degrees that sum to less than or equal to `limit`
   185→  Finset.Iic (degreeMultiset G)
   186→    |>.filter (fun S ↦ Multiset.sum S ≤ limit)
   187→    |>.sup Multiset.card
   188→
   189→/--
   190→Computes the annihilation number of a graph G.
   191→
   192→It calculates the degree sequence, sorts it ascendingly, and finds the largest prefix length 'k'
   193→(where `0 ≤ k ≤ |V(G)|`) such that the sum of the prefix is less than or equal to the sum of the corresponding suffix.
   194→-/
   195→noncomputable def annihilationNumber' (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   196→  -- 1. Get the degree sequence sorted in ascending order.
   197→  -- G.degree_list returns the list of degrees.
   198→  letI degrees := (Finset.univ.image fun v => G.degree v).sort (· ≤ ·)
   199→
   200→  -- 2. Define the condition for the annihilation number.
   201→  -- k represents the number of smallest degrees considered (the length of the prefix).
   202→  letI condition (k : ℕ) : Bool := (degrees.take k).sum ≤ (degrees.drop k).sum
   203→
   204→  -- 3. Find the maximum k in {0, ..., n} satisfying the condition.
   205→  -- List.range (n + 1) generates the list [0, 1, ..., n].
   206→  letI candidates := (List.range (Fintype.card α + 1)).filter condition
   207→
   208→  -- 4. Return the maximum candidate.
   209→  -- The list of candidates is guaranteed to be non-empty because k=0 always satisfies
   210→  -- the condition (0 ≤ sum of all degrees).
   211→  candidates.getLast!
   212→
   213→set_option linter.unusedSectionVars false in
   214→-- TODO(Paul-Lez): debug the issue with the unused variable linter...
   215→proof_wanted annihilationNumberEq (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] :
   216→    annihilationNumber G = annihilationNumber' G
   217→
   218→/-!
   219→## Residue
   220→The residue of a graph is the number of zeros remaining after iteratively applying the Havel-Hakimi
   221→algorithm to the degree sequence until all remaining degrees are zero.
   222→-/
   223→
   224→/--
   225→Helper function: Performs one step of the Havel-Hakimi reduction on a degree sequence.
   226→Assumes the input list `s` is sorted descending.
   227→Removes the first element `d`, decrements the next `d` elements by 1, and re-sorts the list descending.
   228→
   229→Note: when `s` is the list of vertices arising from a simple graph, if the first index is `s` then
   230→the degree list always has length at least `s+1` so this makes sense.
   231→-/
   232→private def havelHakimiStep (s : List ℕ) : List ℕ :=
   233→  match s with
   234→  | [] => []
   235→  | d :: rest =>
   236→    -- Split the rest into the part to decrement (first d elements) and the remaining part.
   237→    let (to_decrement, remaining) := rest.splitAt d
   238→    -- Decrement the elements
   239→    let decremented := to_decrement.map (· - 1)
   240→    -- Combine and re-sort descending.
   241→    (decremented ++ remaining).mergeSort (· ≥ ·)
   242→
   243→/--
   244→Auxiliary function to calculate the residue recursively.
   245→Applies Havel-Hakimi steps until the sequence consists only of zeros or is empty.
   246→-/
   247→private partial def residueAux : List ℕ → ℕ
   248→  | [] => 0        -- Empty sequence, residue is 0.
   249→  | 0 :: s => 1 + s.length -- If the largest degree is 0 (and the list is sorted), all are 0.
   250→  | s => residueAux (havelHakimiStep s) -- Apply one reduction step and recurse.
   251→
   252→/--
   253→Computes the residue of a graph G, ,i.e. the number of zeros remaining after iteratively applying the Havel-Hakimi
   254→algorithm to the degree sequence until all remaining degrees are zero.
   255→Starts with the descending degree sequence and applies the Havel-Hakimi process.
   256→-/
   257→noncomputable def residue (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   258→  -- Get the degree sequence sorted in descending order and apply `residueAux`.
   259→  residueAux ((Finset.univ.image fun v => G.degree v).sort (· ≥ ·))
   260→
   261→/--
   262→Fractional alpha. This is defined as
   263→$$
   264→\max_x \sum_{v \in V} x_v
   265→$$
   266→subject to $0 \le x_v \le 1$ for all $v \in V$ and
   267→$x_u + x_v \le 1$ whenever $(u, v)$ are connected by an edge.
   268→-/
   269→noncomputable def fractionalAlpha (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
   270→  sSup {(∑ i, x i) | (x : α → ℝ) (_ : ∀ v, x v ∈ Set.Icc 0 1)
   271→    (_ : ∀ u v, G.Adj u v → x u + x v ≤ 1)}
   272→
   273→/--
   274→Lovász Theta Function (ϑ(G))
   275→The Lovász theta function is defined as:
   276→ϑ(G) = min λ_max(A)
   277→where the minimum is taken over all symmetric matrices A such that:
   278→
   279→A_ij = 1 for all i = j (diagonal entries are 1)
   280→A_ij = 0 for all {i,j} ∈ E (entries corresponding to edges are 0)
   281→A is positive semidefinite
   282→
   283→Here λ_max(A) denotes the maximum eigenvalue of A.
   284→-/
   285→noncomputable def lovaczThetaFunction
   286→    (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
   287→  sInf {(Matrix.IsHermitian.maxEigenvalue hA) | (A : Matrix α α ℝ) (hA : A.IsHermitian)
   288→      (_ : ∀ i, A i i = 1) (_ : ∀ i j, G.Adj i j → A i j = 0)}
   289→
   290→/--
   291→Given a simple graph `G` with adjacency matrix `A`, this is the number `n₀ + min n₊ n₋` where:
   292→- `n₀` is the multiplicity of zero as an eigenvalue of `A`
   293→- `n₊` is the number of positive eigenvalues of `A` (counting multiplicities)
   294→- `n₋` is the number of negative eigenvalues of `A` (counting multiplicities)
   295→-/
   296→noncomputable def cvetkovic (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   297→  letI A : Matrix α α ℝ := G.adjMatrix ℝ
   298→  letI spectrum : Multiset ℝ := (Matrix.charpoly A).roots
   299→  letI positive_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 < x)
   300→  letI negative_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 > x)
   301→  letI zero_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 = x)
   302→  zero_count + min positive_count negative_count
   303→
   304→end SimpleGraph
   305→

    36→/-- A Sidon set is a set, such that such that all pairwise sums of elements are distinct apart from
